summary:
Geriatrics

Older adults (typically 65+ years) comprise a growing proportion of the EMS patient population. Caring for geriatric patients requires an understanding of the physiologic changes of aging, the atypical presentation of illness, and the unique social and ethical considerations that arise in this population. The key principle: never assume symptoms are "just old age." Treat every complaint as a potentially treatable medical condition until proven otherwise.

Physiologic Changes of Aging

Aging affects every organ system, reducing the body's reserve capacity and ability to compensate for stressors. These changes alter how illness and injury present, how medications work, and how the body responds to treatment:

SystemPhysiologic ChangeClinical Implications for EMS
CardiovascularDecreased cardiac output, reduced maximum heart rate, stiffened arteries, decreased baroreceptor sensitivityLess able to increase HR during stress; orthostatic hypotension common; higher risk of syncope and falls; "silent" MI (no chest pain); heart failure more common
RespiratoryDecreased lung compliance, weaker respiratory muscles, reduced cough reflex, decreased ciliary clearance, decreased FEV₁ and vital capacityHigher risk of pneumonia, aspiration, and respiratory failure; less effective cough; faster desaturation during apnea; compensatory hyperventilation less effective
RenalDecreased GFR, reduced renal blood flow, decreased creatinine clearanceSlower drug clearance → higher risk of medication toxicity; dehydration more common; limited ability to concentrate urine
NeurologicDecreased brain mass, slower nerve conduction, reduced cerebral blood flow, loss of neuronsHigher fall risk; slower reaction times; prolonged post-seizure recovery; increased sensitivity to medications (especially benzodiazepines and opiates)
EndocrineDecreased insulin sensitivity, altered glucose metabolism, decreased thyroid functionHigher incidence of type 2 diabetes; impaired glucose regulation; hypoglycemia more common
MusculoskeletalOsteoporosis (decreased bone density), decreased muscle mass (sarcopenia), joint degeneration, cartilage thinningFractures from low-energy mechanisms (ground-level falls); hip fractures, wrist fractures, vertebral compression fractures; difficulty with mobility and positioning
IntegumentaryThinning skin, decreased subcutaneous fat, decreased elasticity, slower wound healingHigher risk of hypothermia and hyperthermia; fragile skin → easier bruising and skin tears during handling; pressure ulcers more likely
ImmuneImpaired immune response, decreased fever response, decreased T-cell functionInfection may present without fever; higher risk of serious infections (pneumonia, sepsis); atypical or blunted presentation of illness
SensoryPresbyopia (decreased near vision), cataracts, hearing loss (presbycusis — high-frequency loss), decreased sense of smell and tasteCommunication challenges; medication non-adherence (cannot read labels); increased fall risk; food intake may decrease (loss of taste)
Clinical Pearl: The aging cardiovascular system has a blunted compensatory response. An older patient may not mount a tachycardia in response to hypovolemia, infection, or hypoxia. A "normal" heart rate in a septic or dehydrated older adult should NOT be reassuring — it may indicate the patient cannot compensate any further.

Polypharmacy

Polypharmacy — the use of five or more medications concurrently — is the single most common issue in geriatric medical management. Over 40% of adults over 65 take five or more prescription medications, and many also use over-the-counter drugs and supplements. Polypharmacy increases the risk of:

  • Adverse drug reactions (ADRs) — Shown as a chief complaint. Many calls for AMS, weakness, dizziness, or falls are actually caused by medication side effects or interactions. Common culprit drugs include antihypertensives (orthostatic hypotension), diuretics (dehydration, electrolyte imbalances), anticholinergics (confusion, urinary retention, dry mouth), benzodiazepines (falls, respiratory depression), and opiates (constipation, respiratory depression, delirium).
  • Medication interactions — The more medications a patient takes, the greater the probability of drug-drug interactions. Common dangerous interactions include: warfarin + NSAIDs (increased bleeding risk), ACE inhibitors + potassium-sparing diuretics (hyperkalemia), and digoxin + amiodarone (digoxin toxicity).
  • Non-compliance — Complex medication regimens are difficult to follow, especially for patients with cognitive impairment or limited resources. Patients may skip doses, double-dose, or take medications at the wrong times. Bring ALL medication bottles to the hospital — including OTC and supplements.
  • Beers Criteria — A list of medications that are potentially inappropriate for older adults. While not directly an EMS provider tool, awareness of the Beers list helps in recognizing high-risk medications in your patient's history (e.g., diphenhydramine, benzodiazepines, muscle relaxants, NSAIDs).

EMS provider action: Bring every medication bottle, pill organizer, and supplement to the hospital. Document the names, doses, and frequencies of all medications. Ask about recent medication changes — this is frequently the cause of a new symptom.

Atypical Presentation of Illness

One of the most important concepts in geriatric emergency care is that older adults rarely present with classic symptoms. The aging body's blunted compensatory response and altered pain perception mean that life-threatening conditions often present subtly or atypically. This is a leading cause of missed diagnoses in older ED patients.

ConditionClassic Presentation (Younger)Atypical Presentation (Older)
Acute Myocardial Infarction (MI)Chest pain/pressure, radiating to left arm, diaphoresis, shortness of breathWeakness, syncope, altered mental status, dyspnea alone, "just didn't feel right," nausea/vomiting, epigastric pain. Silent MI (no chest pain) occurs in up to 40% of older adults.
Infection (Sepsis)Fever, chills, tachycardia, leukocytosisConfusion (acute change in mental status), fatigue, decreased mobility, incontinence, "off" or "not themselves." Fever may be absent in 30-50% of older septic patients. Hypothermia may be present instead.
PneumoniaCough, fever, productive sputum, pleuritic chest painConfusion, weakness, falls, decreased appetite, tachypnea alone. The classic triad of cough + fever + sputum is often absent. The only sign may be tachypnea.
Pulmonary EmbolismSudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, hemoptysisSyncope, unexplained hypoxia, weakness, confusion. Pleuritic pain is less common.
AppendicitisPeriumbilical pain migrating to RLQ, fever, rebound tendernessVague abdominal pain or distension, nausea, confusion. Classic migration may not occur. Perforation rate is higher due to delayed diagnosis.
HyperthyroidismTachycardia, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, exophthalmos"Apathetic hyperthyroidism" — lethargy, weakness, depression, atrial fibrillation. May not have typical hyperthyroid symptoms.
HypothyroidismFatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, bradycardiaMyxedema coma — altered mental status, hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension — a life-threatening emergency triggered by infection or cold exposure.
NREMT Key Point: Always consider the atypical presentation in older adults. A fall in an elderly patient is NOT a diagnosis — it is a SYMPTOM. The fall may be caused by an underlying medical condition (MI, infection, stroke, dysrhythmia, hypoglycemia). Always ask: "Why did this patient fall?"

Falls

Falls are the most common complaint EMS is called for in geriatric patients. Approximately one in three adults over 65 falls each year, and fall-related injuries are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality. A fall is never a normal part of aging — it requires a thorough investigation to identify the underlying cause:

  • Assess for cause of the fall: Did the patient trip/ slip (environmental cause) or did they have a medical event that caused the fall (syncope, dizziness, weakness)? The answer changes the workup. Ask witnesses if the patient lost consciousness before falling.
  • Assess for injury: Head trauma (even minor mechanism → risk of intracranial bleed in anticoagulated patients), hip fracture (shortened, externally rotated leg), wrist fracture, vertebral compression fracture. Perform a thorough physical exam including the back and hips.
  • Environmental factors: Loose rugs, poor lighting, stairs without railings, clutter, pets, uneven surfaces, inappropriate footwear. Document the scene — it may explain the cause.
  • Medical factors: Orthostatic hypotension (check lying, sitting, and standing BP), polypharmacy (especially antihypertensives, diuretics, sedatives), visual impairment, neuropathy (diabetic or alcoholic), muscle weakness, gait disorders, Parkinson's disease, dementia, and acute illness (MI, stroke, infection).
  • Long lie: A fall resulting in the patient being unable to get up for a prolonged period (>1 hour) is called a long lie. This is associated with serious complications including rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown), pressure ulcers, dehydration, hypothermia, and pneumonia. Ask: "How long were you on the floor?" Document the estimated time.

Dementia vs. Delirium vs. Depression — The Three D's

Differentiating dementia, delirium, and depression is a critical skill for EMS providers caring for older adults. These three conditions can look similar but have vastly different implications — delirium is a medical emergency, dementia is a chronic condition, and depression is a treatable mental illness. Mistaking delirium for dementia can be fatal.

FeatureDementiaDeliriumDepression
OnsetGradual, progressive over months to yearsAcute — hours to daysGradual over weeks to months; may coincide with life events
CourseSlowly progressive, irreversible declineFluctuating — waxes and wanes throughout the day (often worse at night — "sundowning")Persistent but often cyclical; may improve with treatment
AttentionGenerally preserved until late stagesImpaired — difficulty focusing, easily distractedMay be intact but the patient is preoccupied with negative thoughts
AwarenessAlert but confused about detailsClouded, reduced awareness of environmentFully aware but disengaged
MemoryPrimarily short-term memory loss; long-term relatively intact until lateImpaired recent memory, but may improve when delirium resolvesMay report memory problems but testing often reveals normal performance; memory improves with prompting
SpeechWord-finding difficulty, vagueRambling, incoherent, rapid or slow, unpredictableSlow, monotonous, reduced volume, delayed response
HallucinationsRare in early stages; may occur in advanced (usually visual)Common — primarily visual hallucinations (bugs, animals, people) — often frighteningRare; may have mood-congruent delusions (guilt, worthlessness)
ReversibilityNot reversibleReversible if underlying cause is treatedTreatable with medication and therapy
Critical Distinction: Delirium is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. An acute change in mental status in an older adult is caused by a medical problem until proven otherwise. Common causes include: infection (UTI is the #1 cause in older adults), electrolyte imbalance, hypoglycemia, hypoxia, medication toxicity, alcohol withdrawal, stroke, or head injury. NEVER assume an acute change in mental status is "just dementia."

Elder Abuse Recognition

Elder abuse affects approximately 10% of older adults and is severely underreported. Victims are often isolated and dependent on their abuser, making it difficult to report. As a mandated reporter in most states, you must report suspected elder abuse to Adult Protective Services (APS) or law enforcement:

  • Physical abuse: Bruises in unusual locations (inner arms, torso, face), bruises shaped like objects (belt marks, handprints), burns, fractures, restraint marks (wrists, ankles), and unexplained injuries. The caregiver's explanation does not match the injury pattern.
  • Emotional/psychological abuse: The patient is withdrawn, fearful, anxious, or avoids eye contact with the caregiver. The caregiver may speak for the patient, belittle them, or threaten them in front of you. The patient may not feel safe speaking freely.
  • Financial exploitation: Missing money, unpaid bills despite apparent resources, sudden changes in wills, forged signatures, valuable items missing from the home, or the patient lacks basic necessities while the caregiver has new possessions.
  • Neglect (self-neglect or caregiver neglect): Pressure ulcers (bedsores), dehydration, malnutrition, poor hygiene, soiled clothing/bedding, untreated medical conditions, unsafe living conditions (no heat, no running water, hoarding, fire hazards), and inadequate supervision.
  • Sexual abuse: Genital/anal trauma, unexplained STIs, bruising of inner thighs or breasts, and behavioral changes (withdrawal, fear of being alone with a specific person).
  • Reporting: Document objective findings only. Interview the patient privately if possible (away from the caregiver). Use the same documentation principles as child abuse — describe, do not speculate. Report to APS and/or law enforcement per your state's requirements.

Advance Directives, DNR, and End-of-Life Care

Geriatric patients frequently have advance directives that guide their care preferences. Understanding these documents is essential to respecting patient autonomy while providing appropriate emergency care:

  • DNR (Do Not Resuscitate) orders: A physician-signed order instructing EMS not to attempt CPR in the event of cardiac arrest. Valid out-of-hospital DNR forms must be present, signed, and dated. Some states recognize DNR bracelets or necklaces from authorized programs. If a valid DNR is present, honor it — provide comfort care only.
  • POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) / MOLST: More comprehensive than a DNR. These medical orders address CPR status, level of medical intervention (comfort measures only, limited interventions, or full treatment), antibiotics, and artificial nutrition. POLST forms are designed to be honored in all care settings including EMS. They are typically bright-colored (pink or green) and posted prominently.
  • Living wills: General written instructions about the patient's wishes for end-of-life care. They are not as specific as DNR/POLST orders but provide guidance. Contact medical direction if the only directive is a living will without a signed DNR.
  • Healthcare Power of Attorney (POA): Designates someone to make healthcare decisions if the patient cannot. The POA has the authority to consent to or refuse treatment.
  • End-of-life care considerations: Prioritize comfort and dignity. Manage pain and dyspnea. Support the family. Allow the patient to die with dignity in their preferred setting if that is their documented wish. If no advance directive exists, provide full resuscitative care.
Clinical Pearl: A DNR means "Do Not Resuscitate" — it applies when the patient is in cardiac or respiratory ARREST. A DNR does NOT mean "do not treat." A patient with a valid DNR who has a treatable infection, a fracture, or respiratory distress still deserves appropriate medical care and transport. Only withhold CPR if the patient arrests. Continue all other appropriate treatments.

quiz.q: A 74-year-old female with a history of hypertension and diabetes presents with acute onset of confusion and weakness. She has no chest pain. Her ECG shows ST-elevation in the anterior leads. This presentation is most consistent with:
options: Atypical presentation of acute MI in an older adult
A stroke causing confusion
Hypoglycemia — the confusion is from low blood sugar
Normal aging changes — confusion is expected at her age

quiz.q: An EMS provider is called to a nursing home for an 82-year-old male with confusion. The nurse reports he was 'confused yesterday too' and has a history of Alzheimer's disease. The patient does not know where he is, is unable to follow commands, and is hallucinating (seeing bugs on the wall). The nurse says, 'He always gets like this — it's just his dementia.' What should the EMS provider do?
options: Agree with the nurse and leave the patient at the facility since it is his baseline

Recognize that new hallucinations in a patient with dementia could indicate delirium from infection, electrolyte imbalance, or medication toxicity. Transport for medical evaluation — do not assume this is 'just dementia'
Restrain the patient for the hallucinations and leave him at the facility
Give the patient a sedative and contact his primary care physician in the morning

quiz.q: An 88-year-old female is found on the floor by her neighbor, who estimates she may have been there for 'a day or two.' She is confused, dehydrated, and has reddened areas on her buttocks and heels. What is the MOST immediate concern?
options: She has a hip fracture that needs surgical repair
Prolonged lying ('long lie') can cause rhabdomyolysis, pressure ulcers, dehydration, hypothermia, and pneumonia. She needs evaluation and treatment for these complications
She needs a glass of water and help getting up
She is confused because she is old — this is expected

quiz.q: A 76-year-old man with a history of CHF, COPD, and atrial fibrillation presents with generalized weakness and 'just not feeling right.' His vital signs are: BP 96/58, HR 110 (irregular), RR 24, SpO₂ 89%, and he is afebrile. What should the EMS provider suspect?
options: This is likely normal aging — older people feel weak
Weakness + hypotension + tachycardia + hypoxia in an older adult is a red flag for sepsis, even without fever. Approximately 30-50% of older septic patients are afebrile. Transport rapidly
He is having a panic attack
He is dehydrated from not drinking enough water — provide fluids and reassess

quiz.q: HARD: An 85-year-old female with a valid DNR order has a fever of 102°F, productive cough, and SpO₂ of 85%. She is in respiratory distress. The DNR clearly states 'No resuscitation.' The family asks you to 'do everything' for her. What should the EMS provider do?
options: Withhold all treatment because of the DNR

Provide oxygen and transport for the pneumonia — the DNR means do not resuscitate if she ARRESTS, not do not treat a reversible medical condition. Treat the infection and respiratory distress; only withhold CPR if she arrests
Start CPR immediately since the family is overriding the DNR
Call medical direction to ask whether to honor the DNR or the family's request

quiz.q: HARD: An EMS provider responds to a home where a 92-year-old woman is found by her son, who lives with her. The son reports she fell out of bed. The patient has bilateral wrist bruises (shaped like finger marks), a black eye, and is withdrawn. The son does most of the talking and becomes defensive when you ask to speak to his mother alone. What should the EMS provider suspect?
options: The patient fell out of bed as reported — document the story

Elder physical abuse — the bilateral grip-mark bruises on the wrists, facial injury, withdrawn behavior, and a caregiver who is defensive and prevents private conversation are classic red flags. Interview the patient privately if possible. Report to Adult Protective Services
The bruises are from normal aging — elderly skin bruises easily
The patient is depressed from being bedridden

flashcards.question: What are the key physiologic changes of aging that affect EMS care?
flashcards.answer: Decreased cardiac output and baroreceptor sensitivity → orthostasis, silent MI; decreased lung compliance → faster desaturation; decreased renal function → slower drug clearance; impaired immune response → atypical infection presentation; decreased thermoregulation; osteoporosis; sensory deficits; skin thinning.

flashcards.question: How does an acute MI typically present in an older adult vs. a younger patient?
flashcards.answer: Younger: classic chest pain/pressure radiating to left arm. Older: weakness, syncope, altered mental status, dyspnea alone, nausea, or epigastric pain. Silent MI (no chest pain) occurs in up to 40% of older adults.

flashcards.question: What are the key differences between dementia, delirium, and depression (the 3 D's)?
flashcards.answer: Dementia: gradual onset, progressive, irreversible, alert but confused, short-term memory loss. Delirium: ACUTE onset (hours-days), fluctuating, impaired attention, common visual hallucinations — a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Depression: gradual, withdrawn, slow speech, fully aware but disengaged, treatable.

flashcards.question: What is the most common cause of falls in the elderly and how should they be assessed?
flashcards.answer: Falls are a symptom, not a diagnosis. Causes include orthostatic hypotension, polypharmacy, environmental hazards, visual impairment, neuropathy, and acute illness (MI, infection, stroke). Always ask: Why did this patient fall? Assess for injury, mechanism, and underlying medical cause. Document long lie time.

flashcards.question: What is polypharmacy and why is it important in geriatric EMS?
flashcards.answer: Polypharmacy is the use of 5+ medications concurrently. It increases risk of adverse drug reactions, interactions, and non-compliance. Common culprit drugs: antihypertensives (orthostasis), diuretics (dehydration), benzodiazepines (falls), anticholinergics (confusion). Always bring ALL meds to the hospital.

flashcards.question: What types of elder abuse must an EMS provider report?
flashcards.answer: Physical abuse (patterned bruising, fractures), emotional abuse (withdrawn, fearful), financial exploitation, neglect (pressure ulcers, dehydration, poor hygiene, unsafe conditions), and sexual abuse. EMS providers are mandated reporters in most states — report to Adult Protective Services.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between a DNR order and a POLST form?
flashcards.answer: A DNR only addresses CPR status — no resuscitation if the patient arrests. A POLST form covers CPR status PLUS level of medical interventions (comfort only, limited, full), antibiotics, and artificial nutrition. POLST forms are more comprehensive and are active in all care settings.

flashcards.question: A 78-year-old patient with a history of dementia presents with new-onset confusion that started 6 hours ago. Family says she was 'fine this morning.' What should the EMS provider suspect?
flashcards.answer: DELIRIUM — acute onset of confusion in an older adult is a medical emergency until proven otherwise. Common causes include UTI (most common), electrolyte imbalance, hypoglycemia, hypoxia, medication toxicity, or stroke. Never assume this is 'just dementia.' Transport for evaluation.

critical.scenario: You are dispatched to a private residence for an 80-year-old male with 'weakness.' Upon arrival, the patient's wife meets you and says, 'He's just not himself today. He's been in the bathroom all morning with diarrhea, and he can barely walk back to bed.' The patient is sitting on the edge of the bed, pale, and appears fatigued. He tells you, 'I just feel weak. I think I have a stomach bug.' His vital signs are: BP 88/54, HR 106, RR 22, SpO₂ 94% on room air, temp 98.1°F. He has no chest pain, no dyspnea, and no focal neurological deficits. His past medical history includes diabetes, hypertension, and coronary artery disease with a stent placed three years ago. He takes metformin, lisinopril, and aspirin. His wife mentions he seems 'a little confused' — he got lost walking to the bathroom in their home of 40 years.
critical.question: This patient has weakness, diarrhea, hypotension, tachycardia, and new confusion. Is this 'just a stomach bug' or something more serious? What should the EMS provider do?
options: This is likely viral gastroenteritis — provide oral rehydration and recommend he follow up with his PCP if symptoms persist

This presentation is highly concerning for an atypical presentation of acute MI or sepsis. In older adults, GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea) can be the ONLY symptom of an MI, and infection may present with confusion and weakness without fever. The hypotension and tachycardia are concerning for cardiogenic or distributive shock. Apply oxygen, establish IV access if available, monitor ECG, and transport emergently. Do not be reassured by the absence of chest pain or fever
Give him an antiemetic and monitor him at home since his vitals are stable enough
Call his primary care doctor for advice before making a transport decision

critical.scenario: You respond to an assisted living facility for a 90-year-old female with a 'change in mental status.' The nursing assistant reports that the patient was 'fine yesterday' but this morning she is 'agitated and seeing things that aren't there.' The patient has a history of Alzheimer's disease, hypertension, and recurrent UTIs. She is on donepezil, lisinopril, and a baby aspirin. On exam, the patient is awake but does not know where she is or what year it is. She is picking at the air and says there are 'spiders on the ceiling.' She is easily distracted and cannot focus on your questions. The nursing assistant says, 'She has dementia — she gets like this sometimes. She probably just needs her sleeping pill.' You note that the patient's skin is warm, her pulse is 104, and there is a faint odor of urine in the room. The patient's medical record indicates she had a UTI three months ago that required hospitalization.
critical.question: This patient has an acute change in mental status with visual hallucinations, tachycardia, and a history of recurrent UTIs. Is this 'just dementia' or something else?
options: The nursing assistant is right — dementia patients fluctuate and this is her baseline. Administer a sleeping pill and leave her in the facility

This is DELIRIUM, not dementia. The acute onset (hours-days), fluctuating attention, visual hallucinations, and tachycardia point to delirium, not the patient's chronic dementia. The most common cause of delirium in older adults is UTI, which this patient has a history of. Transport for medical evaluation including urinalysis and infection workup. Do not let the nursing staff dismiss this as 'just dementia' — delirium is a medical emergency
The hallucinations are clearly from her Alzheimer's — this is end-stage dementia and she needs hospice, not the hospital
Give her oxygen and see if the hallucinations resolve before deciding on transport

critical.scenario: You are called to a home for a 79-year-old female who fell. She is on the floor in her living room, alert and oriented, with her left leg shortened and externally rotated. She reports she 'just tripped over the rug' but also admits she felt dizzy and lightheaded just before the fall. She tells you she has been feeling more tired than usual and has had some 'indigestion' over the past few days. Her past medical history includes diabetes, hypertension, and osteoporosis. Her medications include metformin, enalapril, hydrochlorothiazide, alendronate, and atorvastatin. When you try to sit her up, she experiences severe pain in her left hip. Vital signs: BP 100/62, HR 92, RR 18, SpO₂ 96%. You suspect a hip fracture and begin immobilizing her. While packaging her for transport, she says, 'I don't want to go to the hospital — I just need to rest and I'll be fine.' Her daughter, who just arrived, tells you, 'She always says that. Don't listen to her — she needs help.'
critical.question: This patient has a likely hip fracture, a reported prodrome of dizziness and fatigue, and a history of cardiac risk factors. What is the EMS provider's complete management plan?
options: This is just a mechanical fall with a hip fracture. Splint the leg and transport for surgical repair. The dizziness is probably from getting up too fast

The fall may have been caused by a medical event (dizziness, fatigue, indigestion — which could be an atypical MI presentation). The patient has multiple cardiac risk factors and is on a diuretic (HCTZ) which can cause orthostasis and electrolyte abnormalities. Immobilize the hip fracture, transport the patient (she has a suspected fracture and requires treatment — this is not a refusal situation for a fracture). En route, monitor vitals, check blood glucose, and consider the possibility of an acute coronary syndrome or electrolyte imbalance causing the pre-fall dizziness. The hip fracture is the INJURY — the underlying cause of the fall still needs to be determined
Let the patient refuse since she is alert and oriented — have her sign a refusal and arrange for home health follow-up
Give her pain medication for the hip, leave her at home, and have her see an orthopedist tomorrow
